title: Emergency Guide: Dog Seizures Lasting More Than Two Minutes
meta_desc: A dog seizure lasting over two minutes is a critical emergency. Learn the immediate steps to take and why this requires urgent veterinary care.
content_html:
What is a Prolonged Seizure?

A seizure is a sudden, uncontrolled surge of electrical activity in your dog's brain. While any seizure is distressing to witness, one that lasts longer than two minutes is particularly dangerous. This duration suggests the brain may be unable to stop the seizure activity on its own, which can lead to a condition known as status epilepticus.

Urgency Level: High (Critical Emergency)

If your dog is currently experiencing a seizure that has lasted more than two minutes, this is a life-threatening medical emergency. You must contact your nearest emergency veterinarian immediately. Prolonged seizures can cause a dangerous rise in body temperature (hyperthermia), leading to permanent brain damage or multi-organ failure. Immediate medical intervention is required to stop the seizure activity and stabilize your pet.

How a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If there is another person available to help, having them record a short video of the seizure can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary team. Visual evidence allows the vet to see the specific movements and level of consciousness, which helps them differentiate between types of neurological events and determine the most effective emergency treatment plan upon your arrival.

faqs.question: What should I do to keep my dog safe during the seizure?
faqs.answer: Move any furniture or sharp objects away from your dog to prevent injury. Do not put your hands near their mouth, as they may accidentally bite. Keep the room quiet, dim the lights, and speak in a calm, soothing voice.

faqs.question: Should I wait for the seizure to end before leaving for the vet?
faqs.answer: No. If the seizure has lasted more than two minutes, do not wait. Call the emergency clinic to let them know you are on your way, safely wrap your dog in a thick blanket for transport, and head to the hospital immediately.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog any medication at home to stop it?
faqs.answer: Never administer human medications or oral medications while a dog is seizing. This can lead to choking or toxicity. Only use medications specifically prescribed by your veterinarian for this exact scenario.
